title: Regular Expressions
summary: Bash supports regex matching natively inside [[ ]] using the =~ operator. Matches are stored in the BASH_REMATCH array. This avoids piping to grep for simple pattern checks in scripts.
keyPoints: =~ tests a string against an ERE (Extended Regular Expression) inside [[ ]]
Do NOT quote the pattern — quoting forces literal string matching
BASH_REMATCH[0] holds the full match; [1], [2], ... hold capture groups
^ anchors to start of string, $ to end
Works only in [[ ]] — not in single-bracket [ ] tests

exercises.prompt: Which operator tests a string against a regex pattern in Bash?
exercises.explanation: =~ is the regex match operator, available only inside [[ ]]. == does glob matching, not regex.

exercises.prompt: Complete the condition to check if variable 'phone' consists entirely of digits.
exercises.explanation: ^[0-9]+$ anchors the pattern to the full string — ^ at start, $ at end, [0-9]+ one or more digits.

exercises.prompt: Write a snippet that checks if a string stored in $email contains an @ symbol using =~, and prints 'valid format' or 'invalid format'.
exercises.hint: The pattern just needs to match @ anywhere in the string.
exercises.solution:
if [[ $email =~ @ ]]; then
  echo 'valid format'
else
  echo 'invalid format'
fi
